metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the CLAUDE.md 'store shares every write immediately' section (lines ~399-408, which hardcodes 'origin/tickets') to the documentation update targets in AC6 alongside the already-listed git-workflow section.
findings.finding: The CLAUDE.md 'store shares every write immediately' section (around line 399) hardcodes 'origin/tickets' and 'ahead of origin' in the ticket-sync context, but the plan only specifies updating the 'git-workflow section' of CLAUDE.md. This second section is an unenumerated doc update target for the same ticket-sync concern.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC6, change 'CLAUDE.md git-workflow section' to 'CLAUDE.md git-workflow section + the \'store shares every write immediately\' section (update \'origin/tickets\' / \'ahead of origin\' to reference the configured remote)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or cite a dedicated `_as_git_remote(v, key)` validator (or a constrained `_as_str` with explicit documentation of what is accepted) for `sync.remote` in `_SECTIONS['sync']`, distinct from `_as_git_ref` (the branch-name validator), matching git's actual remote-name syntax rules (no spaces, colons, tildes, or control chars; dots allowed).
findings.finding: The plan specifies that _SECTIONS["sync"] should coerce sync.remote but does not name the validator. Reusing _as_git_ref (the branch-name validator) is wrong for a remote name: remote names have different syntactic constraints (e.g., dots are valid in remote names like 'gerrit.example' but .lock-suffix components are rejected by _as_git_ref; the path-component loop in _as_git_ref would accept or reject differently than git itself would for remote names). Using _as_str with no constraints is too permissive (spaces/colons are invalid in remote names). Without specifying a correct validator, the implementer will either reuse the wrong one or add an ad-hoc check, risking both false rejects and false accepts.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE works via the existing generic env layer'; src/rebar/config.py _SECTIONS['sync'] and _as_git_ref
findings.suggested_fix: Add `def _as_git_remote(v, key): s = _as_str(v, key).strip(); if not s: raise ConfigError(...); bad = set(' \t\n:~^?*[\\\x7f') | {chr(c) for c in range(0x20)}; if bad & set(s): raise ConfigError(...); return s` — simpler than _as_git_ref since remote names are a single token without the path-component rules.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC5's network-site test to assert that the resolved remote value actually reaches the git subprocess boundary (e.g., via a fixture bare-repo integration test, a captured git-args spy, or a parametrized call-site check) — not merely that `tickets_remote()` returns the right string.
findings.finding: AC5 specifies that 'a network site uses the configured remote' but does not clarify whether that test verifies the resolved remote name actually reaches the git subprocess invocation (e.g., `git push <remote>`) or only that `tickets_remote()` returns the correct value at the config boundary. If the test only asserts the config-layer return value, the threading through push.py/sync.py/fsck.py/init.py could be broken (wrong variable passed, extra hardcoded `"origin"` left behind) without the test catching it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC5 (unit tests) and AC3 (network sites in push.py, sync.py, fsck.py, init.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an integration-style test using a local fixture repo with two remotes (e.g., `origin` and `gerrit`), set `REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE=gerrit`, invoke the relevant command (push or fsck), and assert the git subprocess was called with `gerrit` — or assert the error message names the configured remote. This pattern is compatible with the existing suite's fixture-repo approach and fully exercises the threading end-to-end.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that the push.py presence guard must be updated to check `git remote get-url <configured_remote>` (matching the pattern already used in fsck.py's `_push_pending`) rather than just checking that any remote exists, so the guard and push target are consistent.
findings.finding: The presence-guard in push.py (lines 145-147) uses `git remote` (lists ALL remotes) and checks if any remote exists, then proceeds to push to 'origin' unconditionally. After the change, the guard's semantics and the push target become inconsistent unless the guard is updated to specifically check that the CONFIGURED remote exists (e.g., `git remote get-url <configured_remote>`). The plan's phrase 'presence guard' for push.py is ambiguous about whether it means 'update the guard to check the configured remote' or just 'thread the remote name through the push/fetch commands'. If only the push/fetch/ref strings are updated but the guard remains as `git remote` list-any-remote, then a repo with only a `gerrit` remote (and no `origin`) would pass the guard but silently fail the push because `git push github ...` would return non-zero — the best-effort wrapper swallows it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'src/rebar/_store/push.py (presence guard + push/fetch/ref)'; push.py lines 144-147

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g., `pytest tests/ -k remote` for AC4/AC5; `grep -rn '"origin"' src/rebar/_store/ src/rebar/_commands/` for AC3) so an agent can independently confirm done-ness without stopping to ask.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command is specified for any acceptance criterion — 'the existing suite passes' names no test command, and AC5 ('new unit tests cover…') describes test content but not how an agent would invoke or locate them; an executor must infer which command to run to declare the task done.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('the existing suite passes') and AC5 ('New unit tests cover…')
findings.suggested_fix: Append a 'Verification' section: (1) `grep -rn '"origin"' src/rebar/_store/push.py src/rebar/_store/sync.py src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py src/rebar/_commands/init.py` should return zero hits after the change; (2) `pytest tests/ -k 'remote'` should collect and pass the new unit tests; (3) `REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE=myremote python -c 'from rebar.config import tickets_remote; print(tickets_remote("."))'` should print `myremote`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or a note confirming whether the existing suite exercises git network calls live or via mocks; if mocked, add at least one integration test (e.g., against a `git init --bare` local remote) that verifies a push/fetch uses the configured remote name end-to-end.
findings.finding: The plan's default-path cutover (all git network sites now call `tickets_remote()` instead of the literal `"origin"`) is verified only by 'the existing suite passes', but the plan does not confirm whether the existing suite exercises real git network operations or mocks them out; if mocked, the rerouted default path is never proven end-to-end.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('Every hard-coded `"origin"` network site uses the resolved remote') and AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a validation step (or at minimum a clear error message) in `tickets_remote()` or each network-site call site that checks whether the resolved remote exists in `git remote` output and raises a descriptive `RebarError` (naming the config key and resolved value) if not, before any git network operation is attempted.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no validation or user-facing error handling for the case where `sync.remote` is set to a remote name that does not exist in the local git config (e.g., a typo or stale value). All affected network sites (push, fetch, reconverge, fsck, init) will silently surface raw git errors rather than a clear rebar diagnostic naming the configured remote.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC item 3 (network sites in push.py, sync.py, fsck.py, init.py)
findings.suggested_fix: In `tickets_remote()` or a shared helper, after resolving the remote name, run `git remote get-url <remote>` (or parse `git remote` list) and raise `RebarConfigError(f"sync.remote = '{remote}' is not a configured git remote; check [sync] remote in rebar.toml or REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE")` on failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact location in CLAUDE.md where the git-workflow / remote-layout note should be added (the heading does not currently exist; name the new section or the nearest existing anchor).
findings.finding: The plan's AC6 directs the implementer to update the 'git-workflow section' of CLAUDE.md, but no such section heading exists in the file. Searching CLAUDE.md finds no 'git-workflow' heading and no 'gerrit' mention, so the implementer will have no anchor for this change.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'CLAUDE.md git-workflow section' with a concrete location, e.g. 'add a new ## Git remote layout section to CLAUDE.md' or reference the nearest existing heading (e.g. ## Contributing or ## Setup).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
